title: Employer Accountable Reimbursement Plan Design and Administration

requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must design and administer accountable reimbursement plan from employer perspective to maximize deductible business expenses while ensuring proper substantiation and compliance with tax requirements
criteria: Create written accountable plan document establishing policies, procedures, and requirements
Define eligible expenses - must be ordinary and necessary business expenses deductible by employer
Establish substantiation requirements and documentation standards for all expense categories
Implement reasonable time periods for expense submission (generally 60 days) and excess return (120 days)
Set up processes for expense report review, approval, and reimbursement
Determine whether to use actual expense reimbursement or per diem methods for travel and meals
Ensure plan applies consistently to all employees in similar situations to avoid discrimination
Train employees on plan requirements and provide clear guidance on business vs personal expense allocation

employer_plan_design.business_deduction: Employer deducts reimbursed expenses as ordinary and necessary business expenses
employer_plan_design.expense_limitations: Specific limits apply (50% meals, luxury auto caps, entertainment restrictions, etc.)
employer_plan_design.payroll_tax_savings_score: Accountable reimbursements avoid payroll taxes compared to additional wages or nonaccountable payments
employer_plan_design.compliance_administration: Proper plan administration essential to maintain accountable plan tax treatment
employer_plan_design.per_diem_simplification: IRS per diem rates simplify substantiation for meals ($59-$74) and lodging (varies by location)
employer_plan_design.policy_consistency: Consistent application and enforcement critical for maintaining plan qualification

restrictions: Inadequate substantiation converts reimbursements to taxable wages requiring amended W-2s and payroll tax adjustments
Must monitor and enforce return of excess advances and reimbursements within 120-day period
Cannot reimburse expenses that employer cannot deduct (fines, penalties, political contributions, personal expenses)
Entertainment expenses generally not deductible even with proper substantiation under TCJA provisions
Mileage reimbursement above IRS standard rate ($0.67/mile for 2024, $0.70/mile for 2025) potentially taxable
Home office reimbursements require administrative office exception or specific business convenience requirements
Plan must be established before expenses incurred; retroactive plan adoption may not achieve accountable plan treatment
State tax implications may differ from federal treatment requiring separate analysis and compliance
